item.title: TheBurntPeanut’s Fortnite Arrival Slammed by Gamers as Pride Month Kicks Off
item.content:encoded:
TheBurntPeanut, a divisive VTuber who masquerades as an animated peanut, is making his way into Fortnite as an all-new Sidekick. His imminent arrival has been judged harshly by critics, mostly because Epic Games has given him legs and arms and made him look weird as all hell, but also because many disagree with his more controversial, historic moments.

Clips have started resurfacing across social media of TheBurntPeanut uttering slurs and promoting scandalous stream titles, all of which have been dredged up right at the start of Pride Month.

TheBurntPeanut is headed to Fortnite as a Sidekick, proving how far this animated streamer has come. In the last year and a half, TheBurntPeanut has exploded in the streaming space, racking up a staggering array of followers, coining some irritatingly persistent phrases, and spawning a cacophony of copycat creators.

Unfortunately, his impending arrival, which is scheduled to take place on June 5, has been heralded by many as a foul move from Epic Games.

In a post on Resetera, users started discussing the ‘slur-using’ Peanut, who has been captured in the past mistakenly uttering the ‘f-word’ on stream while playing Escape from Tarkov.

It’s a pretty old clip, but as recently as three weeks ago, TBP hosted one of his regular streams with other creators dubbed ‘Slur Saturday’ on YouTube. Here’s that full stream, in case you wanted a taste of TheBurntPeanut’s content:

Users on Resetera were quick to lambast Epic Games for including TheBurntPeanut in Fortnite. One wrote, ‘Streamers shouldn’t even be on BP in the first place, real people can f*** up at any moment and then you got a skin you can’t remove from the game.’ Another said, ‘Oh, gross, this was the streamer Bungie gave exclusive stream rights to a while back before Marathon came out. That f-slur one is so blatant. Agh.’

One user explained how mods over on the Fortnite subreddit appear to be ‘covering up’ the hate:

Mods are removing posts about this on the Fortnite Reddit, even with the majority of comments in agreement that he sucks.

Do you think this is a lot of fuss about nothing, or are people right to be concerned about TheBurntPeanut’s arrival in Fortnite? Let us know on the Insider Gaming Discord server.

item.title: Raji: Kaliyuga ‘Gameplay’ trailer
item.content:encoded:
Nodding Heads Games has released a six-minute gameplay trailer or Raji: Kaliyuga, the 3D action adventure game sequel to Raji: An Ancient Epic.

Here are the latest details, via Nodding Heads Games:

Raji: Kaliyuga is the sequel to Raji: An Ancient Epic, set six years later. The unsealing of the heavens by the asura warlord Mahabalasura escalates the conflict within the game to a cosmic level. This grand battle encompasses not only mortals but also gods, asuras, and mystical entities.

Raji, now a seasoned warrior, and her dreamwalking brother Darsh journey to the Eternal Summit to save or destroy the universe. Raji: Kaliyuga features dual protagonists: Raji, with acrobatic martial arts and the Trishul, and Darsh, with immense Siddhi powers that control gravity, time, and energy. It introduces customised evasion for Raji and a siddhi-yielding system for Darsh, allowing unique combat styles.

The game is a third-person action-adventure, with Nodding Heads Games committed to delivering a high-quality experience for Raji and action-adventure genre fans. The gameplay trailer was revealed through the prestigious The MIX Summer Game Showcase 2026, a digital showcase event in partnership with Summer Game Fest. The trailer is over five minutes and serves as an introduction into the intriguing universe and characters of the Raji universe, leaning into high fidelity, fast paced action.

Raji: Kaliyuga is in development for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series, and PC via Steam, Epic Games Store, and Microsoft Store. A release date has yet to be announced.

Watch the trailer below.

item.title: PlayStation’s First-Party Sales Collapse Paints a Picture of Concern
item.content:encoded:
In a recent data deep dive, it was explained that, between FY2020 and FY2024, PlayStation’s first-party software sales plummeted by 30 million copies. The sales figures peaked around 2020, with the label securing a whopping 58.4 million first-party sales within 12 months.

By FY2024, that figure collapsed, falling to around 29 million, hampered by a poor release slate and massively underperforming games, like Concord. It’s relatively easy to explain some of these numbers, but with PlayStation’s next State of Play arriving later today, folks are wondering what else the titan has in the bank.

PlayStation 5’s First-Party Sales Aren’t Doing So Hot

In a study published by Game File’s Stephen Totilo, the last six years of financial data were collated and represented in a neat graph, showcasing PlayStation’s first-party performance of late.

Here’s a snapshot of the high-level figures:

  • FY2018: 54.1 million sales
  • FY2019: 49.2 million sales
  • FY2020: 58.4 million sales
  • FY2021: 43.9 million sales
  • FY2022: 43.5 million sales
  • FY2023: 39.7 million sales
  • FY2024: 28.9 million sales
  • FY2025: 32.1 million sales

The sizeable peak secured in FY2020 came courtesy of a few bangers, namely the release of the PS5 console. It also welcomed games such as The Last of Us Part II, Miles Morales, and Ghost of Tsushima, all of which flew off the shelves. Let’s also not forget that it was the fabled year of COVID-19’s global lockdown trend, forcing gamers into their homes with nothing but time on their hands.

The Last of Us Part II, of course, became the gift that kept on giving, with re-releases landing later.

But things remained fairly stable out of the back of that window until 2024 came around.

From FY2020 to FY2024, PlayStation saw a drop of around 50% in first-party game sales, a figure that only recovered slightly last year with the launches of Ghost of Yotei and Death Stranding 2.

Many feel that PlayStation’s push in recent years towards studio acquisitions and live-service titles has been a wasted venture. It’s an initiative that has almost entirely fallen flat, and it has likely cost the company hundreds of millions of dollars, if not billions.

The latest entry to that discussion point has been the 2022 acquisition of Bungie, which Sony completed for around $3.7 billion. In May, it was revealed in Sony’s latest financials that the Bungie buyout had brought about an impairment loss of around $765 million, and that figure continues to grow as Bungie backslides.

Last month, the team revealed that Destiny 2 would be receiving its final update on June 9th, with the studio focusing instead on Marathon, the dwindling extraction shooter, and the ‘incubation’ of future projects.

That’s why all eyes are now on the PlayStation State of Play showcase, set to drop later today, as I write this. It’s the next big thing on the PlayStation horizon, and the hope is that it’ll yield some juicy gems that the label can rely on to drive sales back up. Sony has gone all-in on this State of Play, hosting theatre streams, promoting it long in advance, and pushing for some serious hype.

Stay tuned to Insider Gaming for the latest on the State of Play as it drops later today, and be sure to join the conversation on the Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more Insider Gaming coverage, check out the news that 1047 Games has revealed the first trailer for EMPULSE
